text: The surgeon suspends the left lateral segment of the liver using a Keith needle with silk suture, attaching it to the crura and the right side of the falciform ligament. The surgical instruments are maneuvered with difficulty due to the patient's small size. The surgeon attempts to enter the lesser sac.

The surgeon incises the gastrocolic omentum using a vessel sealer, carefully navigating below the right gastroepiploic artery while avoiding injury to the transverse colon. The stomach is retracted to enhance exposure, revealing severe inflammation and adhesions at the pylorus involving the transverse colon and gallbladder.

The surgeon retracts the stomach further to enter the lesser sac, revealing severe adhesions between the gallbladder and the stomach. The gallbladder is retracted to identify the duodenum, with visible adhesions due to inflammation from a peptic ulcer. The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the adhesions without damaging the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully dissects above the lesser curvature, targeting what appears to be the right gastric vessels. Due to severe inflammation, caution is exercised to avoid injury. The dissection causes bleeding, prompting the surgeon to apply pressure to control it while continuing the procedure using the XI robotic system.

The surgeon examines the splenic vein, noting the patient's abnormal anatomy. Ports are placed below the umbilicus, but the large, redundant stomach complicates the dissection. The lesser sac is entered, and the gastrocolic omentum is incised below the right gastroepiploic using a bipolar device. The dissection continues distally towards the right gastropoploic pedicle, with the transverse colon visible on the left of the screen. The surgeon incises the stomach's attachments to the pancreas, retracting the stomach for better exposure, and observes the right gastroploid vessels.

The surgeon clips and transects the artery and vein using a vessel sealer, completing the dissection of the gastrocolic omentum. The duodenum is visibly adherent to the head of the pancreas due to inflammation. The pancreas is seen above the lesser curve, with the splenic vein visible heading towards the hiatus. The left and right gastric vessels are identified, and the hepatogastric omentum is incised, revealing the inferior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to transect the proximal stomach, ensuring the entire antrum is resected during the distal gastrectomy. Compression is maintained for one minute before completing the transection. The surgeon attempts to separate the pylorus from the head of the pancreas, but due to difficulty, decides to convert to an open procedure.
